item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here and is licensed under an NC "non-commerical" and ND "non-derivative" license. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: Potential Error
item.content:encoded:
"…and when I go inside she acts like I don't even exists."

Do me mean exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd remove the letter and add some more tests. The effect is extremely interesting, if somewhat overpowered. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just want to say that, in my opinion, all ticks are sorrow ticks. Unpleasant little buggers. Liked the general feel of the article though. Might suggest a change of the animal species used from cat/kitten to lab rats and offspring. A cheaper and more standardized test animal without the unnecessary heartstring pulling.

Upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Will keep an eye on it, then. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose. This was my very first article on the site, and it's due for a rewrite soon, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too overpowered / over the top. The permanency really is cheap. Like it or not, the permanency of the effect makes it significantly more boring. And it's the difference between a downvote & an upvote for me. Breaks my suspension of disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great concept. What I'm really curious about is what the effects of SCP-500 would be on the bite victim and the victim's loved ones after removal of SCP-1655 from the victim.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's animal experimentation- it makes sense for the Foundation to do it. It's not about pulling heartstrings, it's about saving resources.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's basically the point. It's not horrifying. It's cheap. The sole reasons for its inclusion is because it pulls an easily accessible heartstring. It doesn't add any new information, it's just repetition of the same old, tired "The Foundation is harsh, yo" message, with a side of "sad baby animals".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Of all the reasons to downvote an SCP, this has to be one of the stupidest.

Killing people for experiments is fine with you, but a cat is too much? SCPs are supposed to be horrifying, for God's sake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted for letting a kitten starve to death.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, not only does it touch on being forgotten, but it also uses a common insect as the harbinger of being forgotten. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is an interesting way to examine the human fear of being forgotten. I like this quite a bit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just weird. I don't find it terribly compelling, though. Also, "contacted us" is a tonal break. Also also, I'm not sure if someone can actually be "previously employed by the Foundation". He would most likely be out looking for D-class candidates or on assignment for some other reason. But I'm also no expert.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There, I made some changes giving additional information. I hope this makes it better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. Ticks live for about two years. I might add some additional information about it's feeding habits, maybe add a feeding procedure.
2. All of the effects are supposed to be permanent. Maybe I should make this more clear.
3. It is saliva. The venom part is a relic of an older version I missed. Thanks for the heads up on that.
4. I wanted to imply the tick uses the victim's memory in order to cause its effect. I might reconsider this.
5. The tick is indeed supposed to feed on mental stress and duress. I need to make it clearer, maybe.

Thanks for the feedback, it's very useful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) How long do adult female ticks normally live, and how many times do they feed? You might need to note that this tick is unusually long lived and/or feeds a lot more often than normal.

2) "These effects appear to be permanent, and will endure even if the parasite is removed. "

You should note whether or not the the "people forget subject if subject gains affection for them" continues after the SCP is removed, or if the permanency only refers to the people the subject had affection for while the SCP was still attached.

3) "Tests of the specimen's venom"

I think this should refer to the SCP's saliva, not venom, but I'm not an expert on ticks.

4) "Though our tests were incapable of confirming how the specimen uses the information it retrieves from the Hippocampus to affect others,"

I don't see anything to indicate that the tick is getting any memories out of the subject, nor that the tick would need to get such memories to do what it does.

5) "it is now clear it is actively causing mental anguish to its subjects, most likely in order to hasten its feeding process."

Feed up the feeding process how? Is the tick supposed to feed off of mental stress instead of blood?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I think I'll get rid of that. It really doesn't fit the tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not a fan of any [EXPLTIVE REDACTED] entries. Everyone at the Foundation is over the age of consent, and just a few days of employement with the Foundation would surely have any researcher seeing things FAR worse than a few "dirty words".

Personally, it turns a relatively good article into a no-vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know about that. It seems a bit excessive to give an amnesiac to every single employee that ever retires. You can trust people to keep their mouth shut. The doctor here (a psychologist) was doing volunteer work in his retirement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems strange that a former employee is allowed to be out and about in the world with knowledge of The Foundation. Wouldn't they be given an amnesiac or something prior to their release?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If anyone has any other suggestions of improvement, polishing or just corrections I'll be glad to hear them. This is my first SCP, so errors of inexperience on my part are bound to exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this a lot, it's effects are supernatural but also possible to catalog and potentially explain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added an addendum discussing the tick's extracts as suggested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Needs some polishing, but on the whole I like the concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The wrath of Wikidot strikes again, feel free to remove this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm having a hard time conceiving of a parasite that affects everyone around its victim.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, agreeing with Ink. It could add some more to an already good article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh bugger, I didn't notice that. I'll fix it right away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a good idea. I'll try and add something like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's ok, I mean, I haven't read anything with this idea, sure it's a tick but it makes it's victim, like, invisible to the world.
I think it's pretty snazzy, and honestly can't think of improvements currently. Upvoted.
Though, you should capitalise the SCP in the title.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like some more study of whatever the tick extracts is called for.
